Senefru.Historical overview: Senefru (also spelled Sneferu) was the founder of the Fourth Dynasty of the Old Kingdom and is considered the father of Egyptian pyramid building. He experimented with pyramid construction more than any other king, building at least three major pyramids: the Meidum Pyramid, the Bent Pyramid, and the Red Pyramid at Dahshur. His work laid the technical foundation that allowed his son Khufu to later build the Great Pyramid of Giza. Senefru was remembered in later Egyptian tradition as a wise and benevolent ruler.

Statue of Ramses II at GEM.Historical overview: Ramses II, often called Ramses the Great, was one of the most powerful and celebrated pharaohs of ancient Egypt, known for his military campaigns, extensive building projects, and long reign of about 66 years. Numerous colossal statues of him were erected throughout Egypt, including at Memphis, Luxor, Karnak, and Abu Simbel, symbolizing his power and divine kingship. One of the most famous is the colossal granite statue originally from Memphis, now displayed at the Grand Egyptian Museum near Cairo.
Statue of Ramses II at GEM.Location: Memphis / Abu Simbel / Grand Egyptian Museum, Egypt

Isis with her child.Historical overview: This statue type depicts the goddess Isis nursing her infant son Horus, known as 'Isis Lactans.' Isis was one of the most important deities in ancient Egyptian religion, revered as the ideal mother and wife, and protector of the throne. This iconic image of divine motherhood was extremely popular in amulets, statuettes, and temple art, and is believed to have influenced later religious iconography of the Madonna and Child.
Isis with her child.Dynasty: Late Period to Ptolemaic Period (various dynasties)
Isis with her child.Location: Various sites across Egypt; examples held in the Egyptian Museum, Cairo

Akhenaten.Historical overview: Akhenaten, originally named Amenhotep IV, was a pharaoh who dramatically transformed Egyptian religion by promoting the worship of the sun disk Aten as the supreme, and effectively sole, deity. He moved the capital from Thebes to a new city, Akhetaten (modern Amarna), and his reign is associated with a distinctive art style featuring elongated features and more naturalistic depictions of the royal family. He was the father of Tutankhamun, and his religious reforms were reversed after his death.

Head of Hatshepsut statue.Historical overview: Hatshepsut was one of the few female pharaohs of Egypt and one of its most successful rulers, known for expanding trade networks, particularly the famous expedition to the Land of Punt, and for her extensive building program, including her mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari. Statues of Hatshepsut often depict her with the traditional attributes of kingship, including the royal false beard, and many were deliberately destroyed after her death, likely on the orders of her successor Thutmose III. Surviving statue heads offer insight into how she presented herself as a legitimate pharaoh.

Amenhotep III and Tiye.Historical overview: This famous double statue depicts Pharaoh Amenhotep III seated beside his Great Royal Wife, Queen Tiye, along with three of their daughters at a smaller scale. It is one of the largest known Egyptian statue groups and was discovered at Medinet Habu in Thebes. Amenhotep III presided over a period of great prosperity and building activity, while Tiye held considerable political influence, reflecting the elevated status of royal women during this era. The statue is now a centerpiece of the Egyptian Museum in Cairo.
Amenhotep III and Tiye.Location: Medinet Habu, Luxor; now in the Egyptian Museum, Cairo

Tutankhamun mask.Historical overview: The golden death mask of Tutankhamun is one of the most iconic artifacts of ancient Egypt and a masterpiece of ancient craftsmanship, made of solid gold inlaid with semi-precious stones and colored glass. It was discovered in 1922 by Howard Carter within the young pharaoh's nearly intact tomb in the Valley of the Kings, covering the head and shoulders of Tutankhamun's mummy. The mask has become a global symbol of ancient Egyptian civilization and is now displayed at the Grand Egyptian Museum.
Tutankhamun mask.Location: Valley of the Kings, Luxor; now in the Grand Egyptian Museum, Egypt

Hanging Church.Historical overview: The Hanging Church, known in Arabic as El-Muallaqa, is one of the oldest and most famous Coptic Christian churches in Egypt, named for being built above the gatehouse of the Roman-era Babylon Fortress, giving the impression that it is 'hanging' above the ground. It has served as the residence of the Coptic Patriarch at various points in history and has been rebuilt and renovated many times over the centuries. The church is renowned for its fine wood and ivory-inlaid screens, ancient icons, and marble pulpit.
Hanging Church.Period: Founded c. 3rd–4th century CE, with later reconstructions

Cavern Church Abu Serga.Historical overview: The Church of Saints Sergius and Bacchus, popularly known as Abu Serga, is one of the oldest churches in Cairo, built over a cave crypt where, according to Coptic tradition, the Holy Family rested during their flight into Egypt. It is a classic example of early basilica-style Coptic church architecture, with a raised sanctuary and rows of columns, and it holds special significance as a pilgrimage site connected to the Holy Family's journey.

Babylon Fortress.Historical overview: The Fortress of Babylon was a massive Roman fortification built to guard a strategic point on the Nile, later strengthened under Emperor Trajan and rebuilt by Emperor Diocletian around the 3rd century CE. It became the nucleus around which Coptic Cairo developed, and several of Egypt's oldest churches, including the Hanging Church and Abu Serga, were built within or beside its walls. Remnants of its massive round towers and walls can still be seen today.

Amr ibn al-Aas Mosque.Historical overview: The Mosque of Amr ibn al-Aas is recognized as the first mosque ever built in Egypt and on the African continent, founded by the Muslim general Amr ibn al-Aas following the Arab conquest of Egypt. It was originally built near his new capital city of Fustat, close to the Babylon Fortress. Though the current structure has been rebuilt and expanded many times over the centuries, it remains an important site of Islamic heritage and continues to function as an active mosque today.
